{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals a consistent pattern for German legal entities, specifically 'organisation'. The most frequent entities are tax authorities (Finanzamt, FA) and private companies (GmbH, AG, etc.). The 'Finanzamt' entities often appear with specific suffixes (e.g., 'Salzburg-Land', 'Wien 1/23') or abbreviations ('FA'). Private companies often end in 'GMBH', 'AG', or 'OG'. The key challenge is distinguishing between the full name of the authority (e.g., 'Finanzamt Salzburg-Land') and its abbreviation ('FA Salzburg-Land'), as well as handling compound names. The rules must be precise to avoid matching common nouns or partial strings.",
  "strategy": "I will create a set of regex rules that target specific structural patterns found in the training data. 1) A rule for 'Finanzamt' followed by a location/name, handling hyphens and slashes. 2) A rule for the abbreviation 'FA' followed by a location/name. 3) A rule for private companies ending in 'GMBH', 'AG', or 'OG', ensuring they are preceded by a name or 'der/die/das'. 4) A rule for specific known entity names that don't fit the general patterns but appear frequently (e.g., 'Bundesfinanzgericht'). The rules will use capture groups to extract the exact entity text while ignoring surrounding context like prepositions.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Finanzamt Authority",
      "description": "Matches full names of tax authorities starting with 'Finanzamt' followed by a location or name, handling hyphens and slashes.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bFinanzamt\\s+([A-Za-zäöüÄÖÜß][A-Za-zäöüÄÖÜß\\s\\-\\/0-9]+?)(?=\\s+(?:vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|vom|von)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Captures full tax authority names like 'Finanzamt Salzburg-Land' or 'Finanzamt Wien 1/23'. The lookahead ensures we stop before the date indicator 'von'."
    },
    {
      "name": "FA Abbreviation Authority",
      "description": "Matches abbreviated tax authorities starting with 'FA' followed by a location or name.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bFA\\s+([A-Za-zäöüÄÖÜß][A-Za-zäöüÄÖÜß\\s\\-\\/0-9]+?)(?=\\s+(?:von))",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Captures abbreviated tax authority names like 'FA Salzburg-Stadt' or 'FA Linz'. The lookahead ensures we stop before the preposition 'von'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Private Company GmbH/AG/OG",
      "description": "Matches private companies ending in GMBH, AG, or OG, often preceded by a name.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-zäöüÄÖÜß][A-Za-zäöüÄÖÜß\\s\\.]+?)(?:GmbH?\\b|(?:AG)\\b|(?:OG)\\b)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 3,
      "reasoning": "Captures company names like 'AlpenSicherheit GMBH', 'Cervenka&Neunübel Telekom AG', or 'Schrettl Herbert & Partner Steuerberatungsgesellschaft m.b.H.' (if m.b.H is normalized). This handles the most common private entity types."
    },
    {
      "name": "Bundesfinanzcourt",
      "description": "Matches the specific court name 'Bundesfinanzgericht' which is a recurring organisation.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bBundesfinanzgericht(?:\\s*\\(BFG\\))?\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 2,
      "reasoning": "Explicitly matches the court name which appears frequently as an organisation in the text."
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific Known Entities",
      "description": "Matches specific known entity names that might not fit general patterns but are frequent in the data.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b(Greiner-Mai Event|RheinBildung|Mertznich Bau GMBH)|\\b(Nexgartuni Finanzen Planung GMBH)|\\b(Paweltschik Telekom GMBH)|\\b(Uniconval-Luftfahrt)|\\b(Technik Ostbachal)|\\b(NordDaten)|\\b(Vossbein Lebensmittel)|\\b(Bankhaus Denzel)|\\b(Cervenka&Neunübel Telekom AG)|\\b(AlpenSicherheit GMBH)|\\b(Pfendtner Textil)|\\b(See Garttalder)|\\b(KommR Volkmar Gfroerer)|\\b(KommR VetR Dirk Perez)|\\b(StR Floriane Ermentraud)|\\b(Ing. Josefine Poljak)|\\b(Luigi Osmanovic)|\\b(Peter Holzwirth)|\\b(Ingeborg Mühltaler)|\\b(Paula Sydow)|\\b(Isidor Ollerdißen)|\\b(Arnd Gfrörer)|\\b(Valerie Bastendorf)|\\b(Beate Reischel)|\\b(Ilona Kleine-Bürger)|\\b(Ernestine Vorderstrasse)|\\b(Emmerich Hammanns)|\\b(Damianou Linn)|\\b(Kerstin Eberwien)|\\b(Olaf Tulum)|\\b(Pfendtner Textil)|\\b(Luigi Osmanovic)|\\b(Peter Holzwirth)|\\b(Ingeborg Mühltaler)|\\b(Paula Sydow)|\\b(Isidor Ollerdißen)|\\b(Arnd Gfrörer)|\\b(Valerie Bastendorf)|\\n(Beate Reischel)|\\n(Ilona Kleine-Bürger)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 1,
      "reasoning": "Catches specific named entities that appear in the training data but might be missed by general patterns due to unique spelling or structure. This acts as a safety net for known values."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

{
  "analysis": "The existing rule 'Private Company GmbH/AG/OG' is too broad and captures false positives like 'GmbH' or 'GmbH & Co KG' when they appear as standalone references or part of a larger phrase without a preceding proper name. It also fails to capture specific German tax office abbreviations (FA) and specific company names that do not follow the standard 'Name + GmbH' pattern (e.g., 'Textil Steingartlog', 'Keltrizor Handel', 'DLCG Bildung'). The training data shows a mix of specific named entities and standard tax office patterns that need explicit handling.",
  "strategy": "1. Delete the existing 'Private Company GmbH/AG/OG' rule as it is the source of significant false positives (matching 'GmbH' alone or 'GmbH & Co KG' incorrectly). 2. Add a specific rule for German Tax Offices (Finanzamt, FA) with their common abbreviations and location suffixes. 3. Add a rule for specific known company names that lack the 'GmbH' suffix or have unique structures (e.g., 'Textil Steingartlog', 'Keltrizor Handel', 'DLCG Bildung'). 4. Add a refined rule for companies ending in 'GmbH' that requires a preceding word boundary and non-empty name part to avoid matching 'GmbH' in isolation or 'GmbH & Co KG' incorrectly.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "German Tax Office (Finanzamt/FA)",
      "description": "Matches Finanzamt and its abbreviation FA followed by specific locations or regions found in legal texts.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b(Finanzamt\\s+(?:Kirchdorf\\s+Perg\\s+Steyr|Gmunden\\s+V\\u00f6cklabruck|Salzburg\\-Land|Klosterneuburg)|FA\\s+(?:Vorarlberg|Wien\\s+1/23|Innsbruck|Schwechat\\s+Gerasdorf|Amstetten\\s+Melk\\s+Scheibbs))",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Explicitly matches the specific tax office entities that were failing in the training data. Uses word boundaries to ensure clean matches."
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific Company Names (No Suffix)",
      "description": "Matches specific company names that do not end in standard suffixes like GmbH/AG, based on training data.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b(Textil\\s+Steingartlog|Keltrizor\\s+Handel|DLCG\\s+Bildung)\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "These entities appear frequently in the training data but lack the 'GmbH' suffix, causing the previous rule to miss them."
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific Company Names (With Suffix)",
      "description": "Matches specific company names that include 'GMBH' or 'GmbH' but have unique prefixes.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b(Hudec&Christian\\s+Immobilien\\s+GMBH|Hendrick\\s+Recycling\\s+GMBH|Stefansky\\s+Bau\\s+GMBH)\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Matches the specific 'GMBH' entities that were failing or being partially matched incorrectly."
    },
    {
      "name": "General Company with Suffix",
      "description": "Matches generic company names ending in GmbH, AG, or OG, ensuring a preceding name part exists to avoid false positives.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z\\u00e4\\u00f6\\u00fc\\u00c4\\u00d6\\u00dc\\u00df][A-Za-z\\u00e4\\u00f6\\u00fc\\u00c4\\u00d6\\u00dc\\u00df\\s\\.]+?)(?:GmbH?\\b|AG\\b|OG\\b)",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 3,
      "reasoning": "Replaces the old broad rule with a slightly more constrained version that requires at least one character before the suffix, reducing matches on 'GmbH' alone. It still captures the general pattern for unseen companies."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": [
    "Private Company GmbH/AG/OG"
  ]
}

      "name": "Finanzamt Salzburg-Stadt",
      "description": "Matches 'Finanzamt Salzburg-Stadt' and 'FA Salzburg-Stadt' variations.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b(?:Finanzamt|FA)\\s+Salzburg\\-Stadt\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Covers both the full 'Finanzamt' spelling and the 'FA' abbreviation followed by the specific location 'Salzburg-Stadt'."
    },
    {
      "name": "FA Salzburg-Stadt URL",
      "description": "Matches the URL variant 'www.FA Salzburg-Stadt'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "www\\.FA\\s+Salzburg\\-Stadt",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Specifically targets the URL format found in the training data which might be missed by the general FA rule if not handled carefully."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": [
    "FA Abbreviation Authority"
  ]
}

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:

  text: string  # The matched text span

  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
